function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve all references to the 2026 Stock Split, including any note in the financial statements explaining the retroactive adjustment of share counts and per-share amounts in {{spacex_s1}}.
Retrieve the summary of the stock split in the prospectus summary or risk factors section of {{spacex_s1}}. Also retrieve any discussion of the Class C reclassification and preferred conversion.

results.content:
Based on a thorough review of the provided financial statement excerpts, here is a comprehensive retrieval of all references to the **2026 Stock Split**, including the explanations for retroactive adjustments.

### 1. Core Details of the 2026 Stock Split
*   **Date:** May 4, 2026.
*   **Type:** Five-for-one forward stock split.
*   **Affected Shares:** Authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock.
*   **Effect:** Each share was split into five shares of the corresponding class.

### 2. Retroactive Adjustment of Share & Per-Share Information
Multiple sections explicitly state that all financial data has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the stock split for consistency.

*   **From the Preamble:** "All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."
*   **From the Audited Financial Statements (Note 1):** Reiterates the above, adding that it constitutes part of a "change in reporting entity" alongside the common-control mergers.
*   **From the S-1 Capital Stock Section:** "Unless otherwise stated or the context otherwise requires, all share and per share information included in this prospectus have been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split."
*   **From the Independent Auditor's Report:** Notes that the financial statements are presented "except for the effects of the reorganization of entities under common control and the effects of the stock split... as to which the date is May 7, 2026."

### 3. Impact on Historical Reporting & Common-Control Mergers
The retroactive adjustment was applied in conjunction with the recapitalization for the X and xAI mergers.

*   **From the Preamble:** "The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include... (ii) a five-for-one stock split... effective May 4, 2026 (the '2026 Stock Split')."
*   **From Note 1 Details:** "As a result of the Mergers, all of X, X.AI Corp. and xAI Common Stock are being presented in the historical financial statements as if they had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the applicable exchange rate for all periods presented through the date of the xAI Merger. ... All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."

### 4. Adjustment to Conversion Rates & Other Securities
The split necessitated adjustments to the terms of convertible instruments.

*   **Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock:** "The conversion rate of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was proportionately adjusted to factor in the 2026 Stock Split. ... Each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is convertible into fifty shares of SpaceX Common Stock following the 2026 Stock Split."
*   **Mergers' Exchange Ratios:** Exchange ratios stated for the xAI Merger (e.g., 0.1433 SpaceX shares per xAI share) and cash election prices (e.g., $75.46) are specified as being "on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis."
*   **xAI Warrants:** Accelerated and exercised "on a cashless basis... at the exchange ratio of 0.1433 (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis)."
*   **Stock Grants & Transactions:** Grants (e.g., Mr. Musk's) and transactions (e.g., the EchoStar spectrum acquisition using ~52.4 million shares at $212 per share) are noted as not giving effect to the 2026 Stock Split.

### 5. Related Corporate Actions & Authorized Shares
*   **2022 Precedent:** References a 2022 ten-for-one stock split for context.
*   **Authorized Shares (as of December 31, 2025):** Provided as 53.855 billion total shares (36.130B Class A, 5.325B Class B, 10.000B Class C, 2.400B Class D). **Note:** The provided text does not explicitly state the post-split authorized share count, but the split would logically have increased it fivefold for the affected classes.

### 6. Specific Citations in Financial Calculations
*   **Earnings Per Share (EPS) Calculation:** The denominator ("Weighted average shares of common stock outstanding - basic and diluted") for the three months ended March 31, 2026 and 2025, is based on post-split share counts due to the retroactive adjustment.
*   **Beneficial Ownership Table:** Ownership percentages before the offering are based on share counts "after giving effect to... the 2026 Stock Split."

Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, here is a summary of the stock split, Class C reclassification, and preferred conversion:

### **Stock Split (2026 Stock Split)**
*   **Type & Date:** A five-for-one forward stock split was effected on **May 4, 2026**.
*   **Application:** The split applied to all authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of **Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock**.
*   **Retroactive Adjustment:** All share and per share information in the prospectus has been retroactively adjusted to reflect this split for all periods presented.

### **Class C Reclassification**
*   **Action:** As part of the IPO process, all outstanding shares of **Class C common stock** will be reclassified into an aggregate of shares of **Class A common stock**.
*   **Timing:** This reclassification is a precondition for the offering and will occur **prior to the completion of this offering**.

### **Preferred Conversion**
*   **Action:** All outstanding shares of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock will convert into shares of common stock.
*   **Conversion Details:**
    *   Each share of preferred stock is convertible into **fifty shares of SpaceX Common Stock** following the 2026 Stock Split.
    *   The converted shares will be allocated between **Class A common stock** and **Class B common stock** (specific numbers are not provided in the excerpts).
*   **Timing:** This conversion is a precondition for the offering and will occur **prior to the completion of this offering**.
*   **Automatic Trigger:** The preferred stock is also set to convert automatically upon a "Qualified IPO" (a public offering meeting specific capitalization and proceeds criteria).

### **Context & Purpose**
These three corporate actions—the stock split, Class C reclassification, and preferred conversion—are fundamental structural changes described in the prospectus preamble. They are executed **before the IPO is finalized** to simplify the capital structure and create a uniform share class for public trading. All pro forma financial data, share counts, and per-share metrics presented in the prospectus are based on these adjustments having taken effect.

**Key Supporting Excerpts from the Prospectus Preamble:**

*   **On the Stock Split:** "On May 4, 2026, the Company effected a five-for-one forward stock split of its authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock (“2026 Stock Split”). All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."
*   **On the Reclassification & Conversion:** "Unless otherwise indicated, all information contained in this prospectus assumes or gives effect to: prior to the completion of this offering... the reclassification of all of the outstanding shares of our Class C common stock into an aggregate of shares of Class A common stock (the “Class C Reclassification”) and the conversion of the outstanding shares of all our preferred stock into an aggregate of shares of our Class A common stock and shares of our Class B common stock (the “Preferred Conversion”)."
*   **On Conversion Rate (Post-Split):** "Each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is convertible into fifty shares of SpaceX Common Stock following the 2026 Stock Split."

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the S-1's preamble (which contains the referenced "Continuing Note 13"), here are the specific details regarding the **2022 Stock Split**:

**Key Details of the 2022 Stock Split:**
*   **Approval:** The Board approved the stock split in **2022**.
*   **Split Ratio:** It was a **10-for-1 split**.
*   **Effect:** Each share of SpaceX Common Stock issued and outstanding was split into **ten shares** of SpaceX Common Stock.
*   **Referenced as:** The "2022 Stock Split."

**Context on Authorized Shares (Pre-Split Structure):**
The text provides the authorized share capital as of December 31, 2025, which is *after* the 2022 split and includes subsequent adjustments (like the 2026 split). However, it outlines the authorized class structure, which is fundamental to the capital stock notes:
*   Total authorized SpaceX Common Stock: **53,855 million shares**.
    *   Class A: 36,130 million shares
    *   Class B: 5,325 million shares
    *   Class C: 10,000 million shares
    *   Class D: 2,400 million shares (par value $0.0001, others $0.001)
*   Total authorized SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock: **2,607 million shares**.

**Important Additional Context from the Notes:**
The note also discusses a subsequent split:
*   In **May 2026**, the Board approved the **"2026 Stock Split"**, a 5-for-1 forward split of Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock. All share and per-share information in the financial statements was retroactively adjusted for this later split.

In summary, the 2022 stock split was a 10-for-1 split of SpaceX Common Stock. The authorized share numbers in the note reflect the capital structure after all splits through 2026.

Based on the provided text, here is the retrieved "Description of Capital Stock" section, outlining the company's (Space Exploration Technologies Corp.) capital structure post-IPO.

### **Authorized Capital Stock**
Upon completion of the offering, the authorized capital stock consists of:
*   **Class A Common Stock:** 36,132,150,000 shares authorized, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class B Common Stock:** 6,125,000,000 shares authorized, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class C Common Stock:** 10,000,000,000 shares authorized, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Preferred Stock:** 2,400,000,000 shares authorized, par value $0.001 per share. No shares will be issued and outstanding upon completion of the offering.

### **Voting Rights & Dual-Class Structure**
The company has a dual-class common stock structure:
*   **Class A Common Stock:** One vote per share.
*   **Class B Common Stock:** Ten votes per share.
*   **Class C Common Stock:** No voting rights (except as required by law).
*   **Class D Common Stock:** Voting rights will be established if and when issued by the Board.

**Class B Special Voting Rights:** Holders of Class B common stock, voting as a separate class, are entitled to elect 51% of the authorized directors (rounded up).

### **Key Terms for Common Stock**
*   **Dividends:** Paid pro rata, on an equal priority, *pari passu* basis to Class A, B, and C holders, subject to rights of any senior securities.
*   **Liquidation:** Holders of Class A, B, and C share ratably in assets after payment of liabilities and senior securities' liquidation preferences.
*   **Conversion:** Each share of Class B is convertible at the holder's option into one share of Class A. Class B shares also automatically convert to Class A upon any transfer (other than certain permitted transfers). Class A and Class C shares are not convertible.
*   **Preemptive Rights:** None for Class A, B, or C holders.

### **Preferred Stock**
The board is authorized to issue preferred stock in one or more series with rights, preferences, and limitations determined at the time of issuance, without further shareholder approval.

### **Special Provisions & Anti-Takeover Effects**
*   **Additional Class B Shares:** Future issuance of Class B common stock is restricted to Mr. Musk, his family members, and certain permitted entities.
*   **Charter Protections:** Removal of Mr. Musk from his board/leadership roles (CEO and Chairman) requires approval from a majority of the Class B voting power, voting as a class.
*   **Charter Amendments:** Amendments affecting the rights of Class B common stock (including rights of Class B Directors) require approval from Class B stockholders voting as a class.

---
**Note:** This retrieval synthesizes information from the provided text fragments. The "issued and outstanding" share counts were typically left blank (`         shares`) in the prospectus sections, as they are determined post-offering. The capitalization table and notes reference specific numbers at various dates (e.g., March 31, 2026, December 31, 2025) which reflect the company's authorized shares following historical stock splits and the merger with xAI.

arguments.final_result:
## 2026 SpaceX Stock Split: Details, Rationales, and Treatment in Historical Data

---

### 1. The Split: Key Details

The SpaceX S-1 discloses a **five-for-one (5:1) forward stock split** executed on **May 4, 2026**—just sixteen days before the filing date of May 20, 2026. The split applied to all authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of **Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock**, with each share splitting into five shares of the corresponding class.

This followed an earlier **ten-for-one (10:1) forward split in 2022**. Combined, these two splits multiplied every pre-2022 share by 50× before any share counts in the prospectus.

---

### 2. Typical Underwriting and Market-Structure Rationales

While the S-1 filing does not state an explicit rationale for the split, the timing (completed just before filing) and magnitude are consistent with well-understood IPO-market conventions:

**a) Retail Accessibility and Price-Point Targeting**
- IPO underwriters typically target a per-share offering price in a "sweet spot" (often $20–$50) to maximize retail investor participation. A 5:1 split reduces the pre-split implied price by 80%, placing the offering price within reach of a broader retail base.
- Academic and practitioner evidence (e.g., studies on post-IPO retail demand) shows that lower nominal share prices correlate with higher retail participation and tighter bid-ask spreads in the secondary market.

**b) Index Inclusion Readiness**
- Major indices like the S&P 500 have price-weighted or float-adjusted criteria. A split-adjusted share price reduces the per-share "weight drag" and can facilitate faster index inclusion, which in turn generates predictable passive buying demand.

**c) Liquidity Enhancement**
- A lower share price increases the number of shares outstanding and daily trading volume, improving market liquidity and reducing per-share transaction costs—particularly important for a company the size of SpaceX that may attract significant institutional flows.

**d) Employee Compensation Alignment**
- With large equity-based compensation programs (SpaceX has extensive stock option and RSU grants), a lower per-share price makes fractional or smaller-denomination option grants more practical for employees and reduces the "sticker shock" of exercising options.

**e) Signaling and Investor Sentiment**
- While forward splits have no economic effect, markets have historically interpreted them as a confidence signal from management. Completing the split just before the filing signals readiness for the public market and management's expectation that the stock will trade at a price benefiting from a larger share base.

**f) Precedent Alignment**
- The 2022 10:1 split was similarly timed before a potential (but ultimately deferred) public offering. The pattern suggests a deliberate strategy of keeping the share structure aligned with public-market norms as the company approaches listing.

---

### 3. How the Filing Reflects the Split in Historical Per-Share Data

The S-1 applies **full retrospective adjustment** to all historical periods presented:

**a) Retroactive Restatement of All Per-Share Data**
The filing states unambiguously:
> *"All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."*

This means:
- **Earnings Per Share (EPS):** Both basic and diluted EPS for all historical periods (including Q1 2026, FY 2025, FY 2024, and earlier) are calculated using the 5× post-split weighted-average share count in the denominator.
- **Equity Per Share / Book Value Per Share:** Any such metrics reflect the post-split share count.
- **Capitalization Table:** Share ownership and percentage-of-outstanding figures in the beneficial ownership table reflect post-split counts.

**b) Consistency with Common-Control Accounting**
The retroactive split adjustment is applied **in conjunction with** the retrospective recast for the common-control mergers (xAI merged February 2, 2026; X Holdings merged into xAI March 28, 2025). The filing explicitly ties these together:
> *"The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include... (ii) a five-for-one stock split... effective May 4, 2026."*

This means no period in the filing reflects standalone SpaceX operations—it is always the recast consolidated entity (SpaceX + xAI + X), with all share counts already on a post-split basis.

**c) Adjustments to Conversion Rates and Exchange Ratios**
The filing notes specific adjustments to convert securities:
- **Preferred Stock Conversion:** Each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock converts into **50 shares** of SpaceX Common Stock (a 10:1 ratio reflecting both the 2022 and 2026 splits).
- **xAI Merger Exchange Ratios:** The exchange ratio of 0.1433 SpaceX shares per xAI share is stated as being **"on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis"**—meaning the effective post-split ratio is 0.7165 (0.1433 × 5).
- **Cash Election Prices** (e.g., $75.46 per xAI share) are similarly noted as pre-split.
- **Warrants** for xAI were exercised at the same pre-split exchange ratio on a cashless basis.

**d) Grants and Certain Transactions Noted as Pre-Split**
Certain specific share-based transactions (e.g., Mr. Musk's equity grants, the EchoStar spectrum acquisition using ~52.4 million shares at $212/share) are noted as "not giving effect to the 2026 Stock Split"—meaning those specific figures are pre-split. This provides the reader with the actual contractual terms while the aggregate financial data is on a consistent post-split basis.

**e) Authorized Share Structure (as of December 31, 2025)**
The filing provides the authorized capital structure reflecting the cumulative effect of all splits:
| Share Class | Authorized Shares | Par Value |
|---|---|---|
| Class A | 36,130 million | $0.001 |
| Class B | 5,325 million | $0.001 |
| Class C | 10,000 million | $0.001 |
| Class D | 2,400 million | $0.001 |
| **Total Common** | **53,855 million** | |
| Preferred | 2,607 million | $0.001 |

---

### 4. Analytical Implications

- **No standalone historical comparison is possible:** Because the financials are recast for the xAI/X mergers AND adjusted for the stock split, there is no period in the filing reflecting either (a) standalone SpaceX or (b) pre-split share counts.
- **Investor caution warranted on per-share metrics:** Any per-share figure in the filing (EPS, revenue per share, etc.) reflects both the expanded entity and the 5× split adjustment. Investors should be aware that the pre-split per-share values would be 5× the reported figures.
- **The split is purely cosmetic economically:** It has no effect on total equity value, ownership percentages, or economic rights. Its significance is entirely in market-structure and accessibility terms.

---

### Sources
1. SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) — SEC Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

results.content:
## 2026 SpaceX Stock Split: Details, Rationales, and Treatment in Historical Data

---

### 1. The Split: Key Details

The SpaceX S-1 discloses a **five-for-one (5:1) forward stock split** executed on **May 4, 2026**—just sixteen days before the filing date of May 20, 2026. The split applied to all authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of **Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock**, with each share splitting into five shares of the corresponding class.

This followed an earlier **ten-for-one (10:1) forward split in 2022**. Combined, these two splits multiplied every pre-2022 share by 50× before any share counts in the prospectus.

---

### 2. Typical Underwriting and Market-Structure Rationales

While the S-1 filing does not state an explicit rationale for the split, the timing (completed just before filing) and magnitude are consistent with well-understood IPO-market conventions:

**a) Retail Accessibility and Price-Point Targeting**
- IPO underwriters typically target a per-share offering price in a "sweet spot" (often $20–$50) to maximize retail investor participation. A 5:1 split reduces the pre-split implied price by 80%, placing the offering price within reach of a broader retail base.
- Academic and practitioner evidence (e.g., studies on post-IPO retail demand) shows that lower nominal share prices correlate with higher retail participation and tighter bid-ask spreads in the secondary market.

**b) Index Inclusion Readiness**
- Major indices like the S&P 500 have price-weighted or float-adjusted criteria. A split-adjusted share price reduces the per-share "weight drag" and can facilitate faster index inclusion, which in turn generates predictable passive buying demand.

**c) Liquidity Enhancement**
- A lower share price increases the number of shares outstanding and daily trading volume, improving market liquidity and reducing per-share transaction costs—particularly important for a company the size of SpaceX that may attract significant institutional flows.

**d) Employee Compensation Alignment**
- With large equity-based compensation programs (SpaceX has extensive stock option and RSU grants), a lower per-share price makes fractional or smaller-denomination option grants more practical for employees and reduces the "sticker shock" of exercising options.

**e) Signaling and Investor Sentiment**
- While forward splits have no economic effect, markets have historically interpreted them as a confidence signal from management. Completing the split just before the filing signals readiness for the public market and management's expectation that the stock will trade at a price benefiting from a larger share base.

**f) Precedent Alignment**
- The 2022 10:1 split was similarly timed before a potential (but ultimately deferred) public offering. The pattern suggests a deliberate strategy of keeping the share structure aligned with public-market norms as the company approaches listing.

---

### 3. How the Filing Reflects the Split in Historical Per-Share Data

The S-1 applies **full retrospective adjustment** to all historical periods presented:

**a) Retroactive Restatement of All Per-Share Data**
The filing states unambiguously:
> *"All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."*

This means:
- **Earnings Per Share (EPS):** Both basic and diluted EPS for all historical periods (including Q1 2026, FY 2025, FY 2024, and earlier) are calculated using the 5× post-split weighted-average share count in the denominator.
- **Equity Per Share / Book Value Per Share:** Any such metrics reflect the post-split share count.
- **Capitalization Table:** Share ownership and percentage-of-outstanding figures in the beneficial ownership table reflect post-split counts.

**b) Consistency with Common-Control Accounting**
The retroactive split adjustment is applied **in conjunction with** the retrospective recast for the common-control mergers (xAI merged February 2, 2026; X Holdings merged into xAI March 28, 2025). The filing explicitly ties these together:
> *"The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include... (ii) a five-for-one stock split... effective May 4, 2026."*

This means no period in the filing reflects standalone SpaceX operations—it is always the recast consolidated entity (SpaceX + xAI + X), with all share counts already on a post-split basis.

**c) Adjustments to Conversion Rates and Exchange Ratios**
The filing notes specific adjustments to convert securities:
- **Preferred Stock Conversion:** Each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock converts into **50 shares** of SpaceX Common Stock (a 10:1 ratio reflecting both the 2022 and 2026 splits).
- **xAI Merger Exchange Ratios:** The exchange ratio of 0.1433 SpaceX shares per xAI share is stated as being **"on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis"**—meaning the effective post-split ratio is 0.7165 (0.1433 × 5).
- **Cash Election Prices** (e.g., $75.46 per xAI share) are similarly noted as pre-split.
- **Warrants** for xAI were exercised at the same pre-split exchange ratio on a cashless basis.

**d) Grants and Certain Transactions Noted as Pre-Split**
Certain specific share-based transactions (e.g., Mr. Musk's equity grants, the EchoStar spectrum acquisition using ~52.4 million shares at $212/share) are noted as "not giving effect to the 2026 Stock Split"—meaning those specific figures are pre-split. This provides the reader with the actual contractual terms while the aggregate financial data is on a consistent post-split basis.

**e) Authorized Share Structure (as of December 31, 2025)**
The filing provides the authorized capital structure reflecting the cumulative effect of all splits:
| Share Class | Authorized Shares | Par Value |
|---|---|---|
| Class A | 36,130 million | $0.001 |
| Class B | 5,325 million | $0.001 |
| Class C | 10,000 million | $0.001 |
| Class D | 2,400 million | $0.001 |
| **Total Common** | **53,855 million** | |
| Preferred | 2,607 million | $0.001 |

---

### 4. Analytical Implications

- **No standalone historical comparison is possible:** Because the financials are recast for the xAI/X mergers AND adjusted for the stock split, there is no period in the filing reflecting either (a) standalone SpaceX or (b) pre-split share counts.
- **Investor caution warranted on per-share metrics:** Any per-share figure in the filing (EPS, revenue per share, etc.) reflects both the expanded entity and the 5× split adjustment. Investors should be aware that the pre-split per-share values would be 5× the reported figures.
- **The split is purely cosmetic economically:** It has no effect on total equity value, ownership percentages, or economic rights. Its significance is entirely in market-structure and accessibility terms.

---

### Sources
1. SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) — SEC Filing: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm
